module.title: Sampling & Chopping
module.description: Learn to work with audio samples the way professionals do — loading, mapping, chopping, stretching, pitch-shifting, and resampling your own sounds to build entirely new ones.

lessons.title: How Sampling Works
lessons.body:
Sampling is the practice of recording a piece of audio and using it as the raw material for a new piece of music. The sample might be a single drum hit, a melodic phrase from a vinyl record, a chord from a keyboard, or a sound you recorded yourself. What matters is that you are working with real audio — captured sound — rather than synthesised waveforms.

Sampling has shaped popular music since the 1980s. The MPC, the SP-1200, and later software samplers made it possible to take any recorded sound and repitch it, loop it, chop it into pieces, and reconstruct it as something new. Hip-hop, jungle, drum and bass, and lo-fi are all built substantially on sampled material. Understanding how samplers work mechanically makes all of those techniques accessible.

## The Three Types of Samples

**One-shots** — a single audio event: one kick drum hit, one snare crack, one stab of a chord, one vocal syllable. When triggered, the sample plays from start to finish (or until the note is released) and stops. One-shots are the building blocks of drum programming and are used constantly in modern production.

**Loops** — a pre-recorded rhythmic or melodic phrase, usually 1, 2, or 4 bars long, designed to repeat seamlessly. Loops have a fixed tempo baked into the audio. If your project runs at a different BPM than the loop, you need to stretch or compress the audio to make it fit — this is where time-stretching comes in.

**Multi-samples** — a single instrument recorded at many different pitches across the keyboard range. Because a sample sounds unnatural when pitch-shifted too far from its original pitch, high-quality virtual instruments record dozens of individual notes and map them across the keyboard. When you play a C, you hear a recording of the instrument playing a C; when you play a G, a recording of the G. This is how most realistic piano, strings, and brass libraries work.

## How a Sampler Works

At its core, a sampler does two things:

1. **Stores the audio** in memory (or streams it from disk)
2. **Plays it back at a variable rate** — changing the playback speed changes the pitch

Playing back at half speed = one octave lower. Playing back at double speed = one octave higher. This is why early samplers changed both pitch and tempo simultaneously when you played different notes — and why there is always a quality trade-off when pitch-shifting far from the original recording.

Modern samplers add algorithms that attempt to separate pitch from time (time-stretching), so you can pitch a sample up without it sounding faster, or loop it without clicks at the loop boundary.

## FL Studio's Three Sampler Tools

FL Studio gives you three distinct instruments for working with samples, each suited to different tasks:

**The Sampler** (the default when you drag any audio file into the Channel Rack) — the simplest option. One sample per channel, with basic pitch, looping, and envelope controls. Best for one-shots like drum hits and short stabs.

**DirectWave** — a full-featured multi-sample player. You can load many samples and map each to a zone on the keyboard. Best for melodic samples you want to play at multiple pitches, and for loading SF2 soundfonts.

**Slicex** — designed specifically for loop chopping. Load a loop, and Slicex auto-detects transients and cuts the loop into individual slices. Each slice is mapped to a Piano Roll note, letting you rearrange, skip, repeat, or pitch individual pieces of the loop. Best for drums, breakbeats, and any rhythmic loop you want to deconstruct.

## A Note on Copyright

Sampling copyrighted recordings without a licence is legally complicated and has resulted in significant lawsuits in the music industry. In practice: use royalty-free sample packs (there are thousands of excellent free ones), record your own sounds, or use synthesis. If you do sample copyrighted material, be aware that releasing a track commercially without clearance carries legal risk regardless of how much you transform the original.
keyTakeaways: Sampling works by recording audio and playing it back at variable speeds — slower playback lowers pitch, faster raises it. Modern time-stretching algorithms attempt to separate pitch from tempo, though all involve trade-offs in quality.
FL Studio has three sampler tools for different jobs: the basic Sampler (one-shots), DirectWave (multi-sample instruments and keyzone mapping), and Slicex (loop chopping and rearrangement).

Loops have a tempo baked into the audio — if your project BPM differs from the loop's original BPM, you need to time-stretch it to fit. One-shots have no inherent tempo and can be placed anywhere on the grid.
lessons.tryThis: Open the FL Studio Browser and find three different types of audio files: a single drum hit (one-shot), a drum loop, and if possible an instrument multi-sample or SF2 file. Drag the one-shot into the Channel Rack — notice it loads as a basic Sampler. Then open Slicex (right-click Channel Rack → Add one → Slicex) and drag the loop into it. Notice how Slicex analyses the loop and divides it into slices automatically. Just observing the difference in how each tool receives the same kinds of audio is the core of this module.

lessons.title: Loading Samples into DirectWave
lessons.body:
DirectWave is FL Studio's full-featured sample player. Where the basic Sampler plays one audio file on a single key, DirectWave lets you map different samples to different regions of the keyboard — making it possible to build multi-sample instruments, trigger different sounds from different octaves, and play melodic samples in a musically natural way.

It is also FL Studio's primary tool for loading SF2 soundfonts, which are compact libraries of multi-sampled instruments widely distributed for free online.

## The DirectWave Interface

DirectWave has two main views:

**Instrument view** — shows a keyboard graphic at the bottom with coloured zones mapped above it. Each zone represents a sample mapped to a range of MIDI notes. Below the keyboard are controls for the currently selected zone: root note, volume, panning, and loop settings.

**Program list** — the left panel shows programs (collections of zones). A single program is typically one instrument. You can have multiple programs in one DirectWave instance and switch between them.

The waveform display at the top shows the audio for the currently selected zone, with markers for start point, end point, and loop boundaries.

## Loading a Single Sample

The simplest use of DirectWave: load one audio file and play it at multiple pitches.

1. Add DirectWave to the Channel Rack (right-click → Add one → DirectWave)
2. In the DirectWave window, right-click the keyboard area → **Load sample**
3. Navigate to a WAV or AIFF file and open it
4. The sample loads as a single zone spanning the full keyboard range
5. Play notes across the keyboard — you hear the sample pitched up and down from its root

At this point DirectWave is behaving like a basic sampler. The sample is mapped to all keys and pitch-shifted based on how far from the root note you play.

## Setting the Root Note

The root note tells DirectWave which MIDI pitch plays the sample at its *original* pitch — no pitch-shifting applied. If you load a recording of a piano playing C4, set the root note to C4. Then playing C4 on your keyboard plays the sample unaltered; playing D4 shifts it up two semitones.

To set the root note: click the zone on the keyboard display to select it, then find the **Root** control below the keyboard and set it to match the sample's original pitch. If you do not know the original pitch, use a tuner plugin or trust your ear.

Setting the root note correctly is important for melodic samples — an incorrectly set root makes every note sound out of tune relative to your other instruments.

## Keyzone Mapping for Multi-Samples

For a more realistic instrument, you load multiple samples recorded at different pitches and map each to a zone on the keyboard.

For example, to build a simple piano multi-sample:
1. Record (or source) recordings of the piano at C2, C3, C4, C5, and C6
2. Load the C2 sample and set its zone from A1 to B2 (it covers the notes near C2)
3. Load the C3 sample and set its zone from C3 to B3
4. Continue for each pitch
5. Set each zone's root note to match its recording pitch

Now each zone plays the nearest recording to the note you press, so pitch-shifting never needs to go beyond a half-octave — the result sounds far more natural than a single sample stretched across the whole keyboard.

## Loop Mode

DirectWave supports looping — playing a section of the sample repeatedly while the key is held, so the sound sustains indefinitely regardless of sample length.

**Loop modes**:
- **No loop** — plays once from start to end. Good for one-shots and short hits.
- **Loop** — loops the region between the loop start and loop end markers continuously while the key is held, then stops when released.
- **Ping-pong loop** — plays the loop region forwards then backwards alternately. Useful for sustained pads where you want to avoid a mechanical repeat.

Setting a clean loop requires aligning the loop start and end points at zero-crossings (where the waveform crosses zero amplitude) to avoid clicks. Drag the loop markers in the waveform display to fine-tune the position.

## SF2 Soundfonts

SF2 is a standard format for multi-sample instrument libraries. Thousands of free SF2 files are available online — pianos, strings, organs, brass, and many more. DirectWave loads them natively.

To load an SF2:
1. Drag the .sf2 file from the Browser onto DirectWave
2. DirectWave reads all the programs embedded in the file and lists them in the program panel
3. Select the program (instrument) you want
4. Play notes — each zone is pre-mapped from the SF2 file

SF2 soundfonts are a fast way to get high-quality multi-sample instruments without building the keyzone map yourself.
keyTakeaways: DirectWave maps audio samples to keyboard zones — each zone defines which MIDI notes trigger that sample. This lets you play a single melodic sample across the keyboard, or build realistic multi-sample instruments from multiple recordings.

The root note is the MIDI pitch at which the sample plays back at its original, unshifted pitch. Setting it correctly is essential for melodic samples — a wrong root makes every note out of tune relative to other instruments.

DirectWave loads SF2 soundfonts natively. Drag an .sf2 file onto the instrument and select a program — all keyzone mapping is pre-done, giving you instant access to multi-sample libraries without manual setup.
lessons.tryThis: Add DirectWave to your Channel Rack and load a single audio sample — try a recorded chord, a vocal phrase, or a piano note. Set the root note to match the pitch of the recording. Now play up and down the keyboard and listen to how the sample is pitch-shifted. Notice how it sounds natural close to the root note and increasingly artificial as you move further away. Then find a free SF2 piano soundfont online (search 'free SF2 piano'), drag it into DirectWave, and play the same notes — hear how the multi-sample version stays natural across the whole keyboard by using multiple recordings instead of pitch-shifting a single one.

lessons.title: Chopping Loops with Slicex
lessons.body:
Slicex is FL Studio's dedicated loop-chopping instrument. Load a drum loop, a break, a vocal phrase, or any rhythmic audio file, and Slicex cuts it into slices at transient peaks — the moments where a new hit begins. Each slice becomes a playable note in the Piano Roll, letting you rearrange, pitch, repeat, skip, and reconstruct the loop as something entirely new.

This technique — chopping a loop — is foundational to hip-hop, jungle, drum and bass, and sample-based production of every genre. The Amen break, the Funky Drummer, countless iconic loops have been sliced, rearranged, and rebuilt into tracks that sound nothing like the originals.

## Loading a Loop into Slicex

1. In the Channel Rack, right-click → **Add one** → **Slicex**
2. In the Slicex window, click the waveform area and select a loop file — or drag a file directly from the Browser onto the waveform display
3. Slicex analyses the audio and automatically places slice markers at detected transients
4. The waveform appears with numbered slices across the top

Alternatively: drag a loop from the Browser directly onto an empty Channel Rack row — FL Studio may offer to open it in Slicex if it detects a loop.

## Understanding the Slice Map

Once loaded, Slicex maps each slice to a piano key starting from C5. Slice 1 → C5, Slice 2 → C#5, Slice 3 → D5, and so on upward. In the Piano Roll, placing a note at C5 triggers slice 1; placing a note at D5 triggers slice 3.

This means you reconstruct the loop — or build something new from its pieces — entirely in the Piano Roll. You are not limited to the original order, timing, or frequency of any hit. A kick that appeared once in the original loop can appear four times. A snare hit can be silenced completely. A hi-hat can be pitched up two semitones.

## Auto-Slicing and Sensitivity

Slicex detects transients automatically, but the default sensitivity does not always produce ideal results:

- **Too many slices**: the algorithm is splitting between the attack and body of single hits. Reduce the sensitivity slider at the top of the Slicex window.
- **Too few slices**: quiet hits are being missed. Increase sensitivity.
- **Ghost slices**: very quiet audio is being detected as hits. Raise the threshold.

After adjusting sensitivity, click **Slice** (or the auto-slice button) to re-analyse with the new setting.

## Adding and Removing Slices Manually

Auto-slicing is a starting point, not a final answer. To edit the slice map manually:

- **Add a slice**: hold **Ctrl** and left-click anywhere on the waveform — a new slice marker is inserted at that point
- **Remove a slice**: right-click a slice marker and choose **Delete**
- **Move a slice**: drag the marker left or right

Zoom into the waveform (scroll wheel over the waveform display) to place markers precisely at the start of transients.

## Triggering Slices from the Piano Roll

Once you have clean slices, open the Piano Roll for the Slicex channel. You will see — or need to create — notes mapped to the slice pitches.

Slicex has a shortcut: right-click inside Slicex → **Make unique** → **Dump to Piano Roll**. This places one note per slice in the Piano Roll at the correct timing to reconstruct the original loop exactly. From there, you can edit, rearrange, or delete notes to transform it.

To hear just a single slice without the rest of the loop: mute all Piano Roll notes except one. Alternatively, click any slice number on the Slicex waveform to preview it in isolation.

## Rearranging and Transforming the Chop

Once the original loop is dumped to the Piano Roll, the creative work begins:

**Reorder hits**: move notes to different positions in the bar. Put the snare on the off-beat. Push the kick to a different 16th-note position.

**Repeat hits**: copy a note and paste it in additional positions. Double-up a hi-hat. Triple a kick for a roll.

**Pitch individual slices**: move a note up or down semitones in the Piano Roll to pitch that specific slice. Moving a snare slice up 3 semitones gives it a higher, snappier character without affecting the rest of the loop.

**Change velocity**: lower the velocity of some notes to ghost them — quieter hits that sit underneath the main pattern create groove and feel human.

**Remove hits entirely**: deleting a note silences that slice. Removing the original kick entirely and replacing it with a different kick from your library gives you a new drum sound with the loop's groove.
keyTakeaways: Slicex cuts a loop at transient peaks and maps each slice to a Piano Roll note (starting at C5). This lets you rearrange, repeat, pitch, and remove individual hits — reconstructing the loop as something entirely new.

Auto-slicing is a starting point: adjust sensitivity to fix too many or too few slices, then add or remove markers manually. Hold Ctrl and click the waveform to add a slice; right-click a marker to delete it.
Use Dump to Piano Roll (right-click Slicex) to export the original loop timing as MIDI notes, then edit from there — reordering, repeating, pitching, and ghosting notes to transform the chop.
lessons.tryThis: Find a drum loop in the FL Studio library (Packs → Drums → Loops) and load it into Slicex. Let it auto-slice, then use Dump to Piano Roll to get the original timing as MIDI. Press Play and confirm you hear the loop playing back correctly. Now delete every other snare hit (every note mapped to the snare slice). Then copy one kick note and paste it one 16th note earlier than where it originally landed. Play the result — you have just rearranged a loop. The original groove is still recognisable but the pattern is different. Keep editing until it sounds like yours.

lessons.title: Time-Stretching and Pitch-Shifting
lessons.body:
When you drop a loop recorded at 95 BPM into a project running at 140 BPM, one of two things has to happen: either you change the loop's tempo to match the project (time-stretching), or you live with it playing at the wrong speed. Similarly, a sample recorded in B minor does not automatically fit a track in C major — you need to pitch-shift it.

Time-stretching and pitch-shifting are the tools that make samples fit. They are also creative tools in their own right — extreme stretching creates ambient textures, and deliberate pitch artefacts are an aesthetic choice in many genres.

## Time-Stretching: The Problem

A sampler naively changes pitch and time together: playing back at half speed lowers pitch by an octave and doubles duration. Playing at double speed raises pitch by an octave and halves duration. This works for pitched melodic samples you want at different pitches, but it fails when you need to change tempo while preserving pitch — or vice versa.

Time-stretching algorithms attempt to separate the two. They analyse the audio and either compress or expand the time dimension while leaving the pitch largely intact. Every algorithm makes compromises, and those compromises are audible — this is why the choice of stretch algorithm matters.

## Stretch Algorithms in FL Studio

FL Studio offers several stretch algorithms, accessible when you right-click an audio clip in the Playlist or in Edison. The most important:

**Elastique Pro** — the highest quality general-purpose algorithm. Handles most material well with minimal artefacts. Use this as the default for melodic content (vocals, chords, pads).

**Elastique Efficient** — faster and lighter on CPU than Pro, with slightly lower quality. Acceptable for less critical material.

**Slice** — does not actually stretch the audio. Instead, it cuts the audio at transient points and repositions the slices without stretching the audio between them. This produces no pitch artefacts on the hits themselves but can create audible gaps or overlaps between slices. Works well for drum loops where the transients are well-defined.

**Stretch (legacy)** — FL Studio's older algorithm, lower quality than Elastique but occasionally preferred for the specific artefacts it introduces. Extreme stretch rates with this algorithm produce the granular, washy textures used in ambient and experimental music.

## Time-Stretching Audio Clips in the Playlist

The simplest way to time-stretch a loop to project BPM:

1. Drop the loop audio file into the Playlist as an audio clip
2. Right-click the clip → **Properties**
3. Enable **Fit to tempo** — FL Studio calculates the loop's original BPM from its length and the project BPM, then stretches it to fit
4. If the auto-detected BPM is wrong, set it manually in the Properties panel

Alternatively, in the Playlist, hold **Alt** and drag the right edge of an audio clip — this stretches the clip's time to fill whatever length you drag to. The clip automatically stretches or compresses to fit.

## Pitch-Shifting Without Changing Tempo

To shift a sample's pitch without changing its timing:

**In the Channel Rack Sampler**: the sample properties panel has a pitch knob measured in semitones. Adjusting this repitches the sample using FL Studio's internal algorithm. Up to ±12 semitones sounds reasonable with Elastique; beyond that, artefacts become obvious.

**In Edison** (FL Studio's audio editor):
1. Load the sample into Edison (drag from Browser, or from an audio clip right-click → Edit)
2. Go to **Tools → Pitch shift**
3. Set the semitones and cents to shift — positive = up, negative = down
4. Choose the algorithm (Elastique for quality, or Resample for the pitch-plus-tempo-change effect)
5. Apply and export or drag back into the project

## Matching a Sample to Your Track's Key

Practical workflow for tuning a melodic loop to your project:

1. Identify the sample's key — load it in Edison, use the tuner, or play it next to your melody and find by ear which note matches the root
2. Calculate how many semitones you need to shift: if the sample is in A and your track is in C, you need +3 semitones
3. Apply the pitch shift in the Sampler or Edison
4. Verify: play the sample alongside your chord progression. It should lock in on the root chord without beating or clashing

## Creative Uses of Extreme Stretching

Pushing time-stretch beyond its intended range produces artefacts — and those artefacts are often musically useful:

- Stretching a 2-bar loop to 32 bars with the legacy Stretch algorithm produces a slow, granular wash with swirling tonal smearing — useful for ambient intros and transitions
- Extreme pitch-down (−24 to −36 semitones) with Elastique turns a short hi-hat into a pitched, textural sound
- Pitching a vocal sample up +7 or +12 semitones with a low-quality algorithm produces a chipmunk-style artefact that some producers use intentionally
keyTakeaways: Time-stretching changes a loop's tempo while preserving pitch; pitch-shifting changes pitch while preserving tempo. Both involve algorithmic trade-offs — Elastique Pro is the highest-quality choice for most material.
In the Playlist, right-click an audio clip → Properties → Fit to tempo to auto-stretch a loop to the project BPM. Alt-drag the right edge of a clip to manually stretch it to any length.

Match a melodic sample to your track's key by identifying its root note, calculating the semitone distance to your target key, and applying that shift in the Sampler pitch knob or in Edison's pitch shift tool.
lessons.tryThis: Drop a drum loop from the FL Studio library into the Playlist as an audio clip. Open the clip's Properties and enable Fit to tempo — if the loop was at a different BPM, you should hear it snap to your project's tempo. Now try stretching it manually: hold Alt and drag the right edge of the clip to double its length. Switch between the Elastique and Slice algorithms in Properties and listen to how each handles the same stretch. Which sounds cleaner on the drum hits? Which introduces more artefacts? Knowing the sonic difference between algorithms helps you choose the right one under time pressure.
